title.en: Compliance Frameworks: SOC 2, ISO 27001, GDPR in Practice

description.en: Understand what SOC 2, ISO 27001, and GDPR actually demand of a multi-cloud estate, how shared responsibility really divides the work, and how to run one control set mapped to many frameworks.

story_variants.en:
## The Question That Stops Sales Cycles

Your biggest prospect's security questionnaire arrives: *"Provide your SOC 2 Type II report, ISO 27001 certificate, and GDPR Article 28 processing terms."* Suddenly compliance is not a back-office concern; it is revenue. And because your platform spans Azure, AWS, and GCP, every answer must hold true in three estates simultaneously.

The first mental correction: **your cloud providers' certifications are not your certifications.** Azure, AWS, and GCP each hold extensive attestations (SOC reports, ISO 27001/27017/27018, and many more) — but those cover *their* side of the shared responsibility line. Your auditor examines *your* side: your IAM, your change management, your logging, your data handling. Provider compliance is inherited scaffolding, not a costume you get to wear.

## The Big Three, Demystified

- **SOC 2** — an *attestation* (not a certification) under the AICPA's Trust Services Criteria: security (mandatory), plus optional availability, confidentiality, processing integrity, privacy. **Type I** examines control design at a point in time; **Type II** examines *operating effectiveness over a period* (typically 6-12 months) — which is why Type II is what enterprise buyers demand. Output: a detailed report under NDA.
- **ISO/IEC 27001** — an international *certification* of your ISMS (information security management system): risk assessment, a Statement of Applicability selecting controls from Annex A (restructured to 93 controls in the 2022 revision), management review, internal audit, continuous improvement. Output: a certificate anyone can verify; audits recur in a three-year cycle.
- **GDPR** — not a certificate at all but *law*, with fines scaling to 4% of global annual turnover. It demands lawful bases for processing, data-subject rights (access, erasure, portability), 72-hour breach notification to regulators, Article 28 processor contracts, records of processing, DPIAs for high-risk processing, and lawful cross-border transfer mechanisms — the machinery you met in the data-residency lesson.

> **Callout:** SOC 2 answers "can this vendor be trusted?", ISO 27001 answers "does this organization *manage* security systematically?", GDPR answers "is personal data processed lawfully?". They overlap perhaps 60-70% at the control level — which is exactly the exploitable fact.

## One Control Set, Many Frameworks

The defining move of mature compliance programs: maintain **one internal control catalog** and *map* it outward to every framework, instead of running one project per framework:

- Control "access reviews quarterly" satisfies SOC 2 CC6, ISO 27001 access-control requirements, and GDPR's integrity-and-confidentiality principle simultaneously.
- Meta-frameworks exist to help: NIST CSF, CIS Controls, or the Cloud Security Alliance's CCM ship pre-built cross-mappings.
- Evidence is collected **once per control**, tagged to every framework it serves. Auditor asks, query answers.

| Concern | Azure | AWS | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compliance posture dashboard | Microsoft Defender for Cloud regulatory compliance | AWS Security Hub standards / Audit Manager | Security Command Center compliance views |
| Evidence/audit tooling | Purview Compliance Manager | AWS Audit Manager | Assured Workloads + Audit Manager |
| Config-as-evidence | Azure Policy compliance states | AWS Config conformance packs | Org Policy + Config Validator |
| Provider attestations portal | Service Trust Portal | AWS Artifact | Compliance Reports Manager |

Multi-cloud discipline: the *control* is canonical; each cloud column is an *implementation*. The audit fails not when a cloud lacks a feature, but when your matrix has an unimplemented cell nobody owns.

## Continuous Compliance or Theatrical Compliance

Annual-audit-panic compliance produces binders; continuous compliance produces evidence as a by-product of operations:

- **Policy-as-code is control-as-code.** The Azure Policy / Config rules / Org Policy guardrails from your landing zone *are* preventive controls — their compliance state exports *are* evidence.
- **Pipelines generate artifacts.** Change management evidence = PR reviews + CI logs + deployment approvals, already timestamped and immutable.
- **Drift is a finding before the auditor finds it.** A control that silently stopped operating in month three of a Type II period is an exception in the report your customers read.

## Real Failure Modes

- **The scope dodge that backfires.** Scoping SOC 2 to only the anchor cloud, while production data demonstrably flows through the others. Auditors follow data flows; buyers read scope sections carefully.
- **Evidence archaeology.** Six weeks before the audit, engineers screenshot consoles to reconstruct a year of history. Type II demands *period* evidence; screenshots of today prove nothing about March.
- **The paper DPO.** GDPR roles assigned on the org chart with no operational reality — no records of processing, no tested breach-notification runbook. The 72-hour clock does not pause for you to figure out who calls whom.
- **Sub-processor sprawl.** Your AI features quietly ship customer text to model APIs not listed in your Article 28 sub-processor list. (This one returns with force in the LLMOps lesson.)

## What You Will Build

The challenge implements the control-mapping engine: one set of implemented controls checked against each framework's required list, printing per-framework gaps. This little loop is, honestly, the beating heart of every GRC platform you will ever be sold — the expensive part is the evidence pipeline behind it, which you now know how to build from your landing zone up.

description.en: One canonical control catalog maps outward to every framework; landing-zone guardrails feed the evidence pipeline automatically.

left.label: One project per framework; screenshot evidence before each audit

right.label: One control set mapped to all frameworks; evidence generated continuously by pipelines

description.en: Frameworks overlap 60-70% at the control level. Collect evidence once per control, tag it to every framework it serves.

explanation.en: Each framework is just a required-control list from the mapping's point of view.
Notice access_control appears in both SOC 2 and ISO 27001 — implement once, satisfy both.
The gap function is the whole GRC engine: required minus implemented, per framework, sorted for stable reports.

description.en: Continuous compliance: the audit becomes a query against evidence that already exists.

lines: next: assign owners to 2 gap controls

description.en: A gap report with owners beats a binder with screenshots — every time, in every audit.

instruction.en: Build the framework gap analyzer at the heart of every GRC tool. FRAMEWORKS maps each framework to its required controls; IMPLEMENTED is the set your estate actually operates. Implement gaps(framework) -> sorted list of required controls missing from IMPLEMENTED. For each framework in the order soc2, iso27001, gdpr print exactly '<framework>: COMPLIANT' if no gaps, else '<framework>: GAPS <g1>,<g2>,...' (sorted, comma-joined, no spaces). Then print 'Frameworks fully covered: <n>/<total>'. One control set, many frameworks — this loop is why.

practice_challenges.expected_output:
soc2: COMPLIANT
iso27001: GAPS asset_inventory
gdpr: GAPS consent_management
Frameworks fully covered: 1/3

en: gaps: return sorted(c for c in FRAMEWORKS[framework] if c not in IMPLEMENTED).
Iterate ORDER, not FRAMEWORKS.keys(), so output order is guaranteed.
Join gap lists with ','.join(g) and count frameworks whose gap list is empty for the summary line.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert gaps("soc2") == []
assert gaps("iso27001") == ["asset_inventory"]
assert gaps("gdpr") == ["consent_management"]
assert isinstance(gaps("soc2"), list)

q.en: Why does your cloud provider's SOC 2 report not make YOUR product SOC 2 compliant?

en: Shared responsibility: the provider's attestation covers their side; auditors examine your IAM, change management, and data handling

q.en: What distinguishes SOC 2 Type II from Type I?

en: Type II examines operating effectiveness over a period (months), not just control design at a point in time
Type II is issued by ISO

q.en: Which statement about GDPR is correct?

en: It is a certification you can frame on the wall
It is law, with breach notification to regulators required within 72 hours and fines scaling with global turnover
It only applies to companies headquartered in the EU
It is satisfied automatically by ISO 27001

q.en: What is the core advantage of one internal control catalog mapped to many frameworks?

en: Overlapping requirements (often 60-70%) mean evidence is collected once per control and reused across every framework
It removes the need for a risk assessment
It makes SOC 2 and ISO 27001 legally identical
